docTitle: UCR guidance
text:
Urgent Community Response UCR what is it two-hour same day crisis response NHS England national standard
NHS Urgent Community Response (UCR) delivers urgent, crisis response support to people in their own homes or usual place of residence (including care homes) within two hours or same day. It is a national standard from the NHS Long Term Plan, building on NICE guideline NG74. The aim is to meet urgent care needs at home, prevent avoidable hospital admissions and keep people independent, using hospitals only when clinically necessary. Integrated care systems (ICSs) must provide a consistent service at scale, a minimum of 8am to 8pm seven days a week, across the full ICS geography, accepting referrals from all appropriate sources and submitting complete data to the Community Services Data Set (CSDS).

docTitle: UCR guidance
text:
operational requirements ICS commissioners providers consistent service geographic coverage operating hours 70 percent referrals CSDS
All ICS planners, commissioners and providers should: provide a consistent service at scale with full geographic coverage; operate a minimum of 8am to 8pm, seven days a week (at least 12 hours a day), extending hours where demand requires; accept referrals from all appropriate sources; and submit complete data to CSDS to demonstrate the two-hour standard. They must achieve, and ideally exceed, the minimum threshold of reaching 70% of two-hour crisis response demand within two hours. They should increase referrals from all key routes, especially Urgent and Emergency Care including 111 and 999, through partnership working with ambulance services, technology-enabled care (TEC), keeping the Directory of Services (DoS) up to date, and accepting self-referrals from individuals and carers.

docTitle: UCR guidance
headingPath: NHS UCR guidance: Working with other services
section: NHS UCR guidance: Working with other services
text:
alignment unplanned care primary care neighbourhood multidisciplinary team mental health virtual ward hospital at home diagnostics district nursing social care reablement
UCR must align with other urgent and planned care: unplanned care (NHS111, urgent treatment centres, same day emergency care, emergency departments, 999); primary care (GP out-of-hours, general practice, PCN teams); neighbourhood multidisciplinary teams (GPs, specialist nurses, district nurses, AHPs, mental health workers, social workers, care home staff, VCFSE); adult and older adult mental health services; proactive care and enhanced health in care homes; community and district nursing (often the core service model); planned care such as hospice-at-home and hospital at home (virtual wards); social care and reablement services; and diagnostic teams with direct referral routes to Community Diagnostic Centres.

docTitle: UCR guidance
headingPath: NHS UCR guidance: Service model and single point of access
section: NHS UCR guidance: Service model and single point of access
text:
design delivery principles single point of access SPoA no wrong door triage interdisciplinary working clock start stop 48 hours telemedicine
The service should be commissioned through a local single point of access (SPoA) applying a 'no wrong door' ethos, giving access to multidisciplinary clinical and non-clinical input. Where a person has already been triaged by 999 or NHS111 there should be no need for further triage. The professional deployed should be matched to the person's needs to avoid repeated assessment. Team members work in an interdisciplinary way. To meet the national standard the assessment and care should start in under two hours (clock stop), with short-term interventions typically lasting under 48 hours. The vast majority of UCR care should be delivered in person; remote responses (telemedicine/video) should only be a comprehensive assessment based on sound clinical judgement and an appropriate risk assessment. Services should focus on reducing health inequalities.

docTitle: UCR guidance
headingPath: NHS UCR guidance: Demand and capacity
section: NHS UCR guidance: Demand and capacity
text:
demand and capacity modelling cost effective service operating model NHS Elect SDEC opportunity identifier review
Demand and capacity modelling helps providers and systems understand demand and plan sufficient capacity so patients do not wait unnecessarily. Tools include NHS England demand and capacity models, training and guidance, and the NHS Elect Same Day Emergency Care 'opportunity identifier tool'. Services should understand the cost of their service and seek to provide a cost-effective service and optimum operating model. Demand and capacity should be reviewed regularly alongside percentage achievement of UCR, with plans put in place to meet demand.

docTitle: UCR guidance
headingPath: NHS UCR guidance: Monitoring, benchmarking and performance
section: NHS UCR guidance: Monitoring, benchmarking and performance
text:
monitoring benchmarking CSDS section 250 Health and Social Care Act performance framework oversight metrics UCR dashboard impact metrics
All providers of NHS-funded UCR are legally required to submit data to CSDS monthly under section 250 of the Health and Social Care Act 2012. Performance is monitored by: consistent delivery for all clinical conditions and needs; delivery at scale across the ICS footprint; delivery 8am to 8pm seven days a week; 70% of two-hour UCR care delivered within two hours; submission of robust data to CSDS; and increasing acceptance of referrals from all sources, especially 111 and 999. The NHS Oversight Framework includes UCR oversight metrics. Impact metrics may include hospital admissions, inequalities data, ED attendance, SDEC attendance, ambulance conveyance rates, NHS111 referrals, care home referrals, self-referrals and onward referrals. The UCR Monitoring Dashboard supports quality improvement and benchmarking locally, regionally and nationally.

docTitle: UCR guidance
headingPath: NHS UCR guidance: Clinical criteria for UCR
section: NHS UCR guidance: Clinical criteria for UCR
text:
clinical criteria who qualifies adults over 18 crisis sudden deterioration stressor event palliative social crisis carer breakdown
Community UCR services are for adults (over 18). A person should receive a UCR response (two-hour or same day) if: they are experiencing a crisis, defined as a sudden deterioration in their health and wellbeing; the crisis may have been caused by a stressor event leading to exacerbation of an existing condition, onset of a new condition, or significant deterioration in clinical state or baseline functioning; and the health or social care need requires urgent treatment or support within two hours that can be safely delivered at home. Crises may be due to a clinical condition (new acute problem such as infection, or exacerbation of a chronic condition), serious illness where home treatment matches the person's wishes under a pre-agreed treatment escalation plan (e.g. palliative care), or a social crisis such as breakdown of unpaid carer arrangements causing an immediate health risk.

docTitle: UCR guidance
headingPath: NHS UCR guidance: Inclusion and exclusion criteria
section: NHS UCR guidance: Inclusion and exclusion criteria
text:
inclusion exclusion criteria two-hour versus same day admission risk dementia mental health crisis professional judgement treatment escalation plan
A two-hour response is typically required when a person is at risk of admission or re-admission due to a crisis and is likely to attend hospital within 2 to 24 hours without intervention. Inclusion: over 18; living in own home or residential/care home; in crisis needing intervention within two hours or same day to stay safely at home and avoid admission; living with dementia (best practice is shared care with older people's mental health teams). Exclusion: acutely unwell or injured requiring emergency care and acute admission; experiencing a functional mental health crisis with no co-occurring urgent physical needs (specialist mental health services should respond); or needs acute/complex diagnostics and intervention for safety in hospital. Exclusions may not apply where a treatment escalation plan, advance care plan or advance decision states the person wishes treatment at home. Professional judgement should always be used, considering co-morbidities and complexity. Where an exclusion applies, the team should find alternative NHS support rather than simply rejecting the referral.

docTitle: UCR guidance
headingPath: NHS UCR guidance: Common clinical conditions and needs
section: NHS UCR guidance: Common clinical conditions and needs
text:
common clinical conditions falls frailty decompensation reduced mobility deconditioning palliative end of life equipment confusion delirium catheter diabetes carer breakdown
A 'no wrong door' approach applies; this non-exhaustive list shows common conditions/needs UCR teams should support as a minimum: Falls with no apparent serious injury where care is needed within two hours to prevent admission ('Level 2' falls), with lifting equipment and manual handling aids available; Decompensation of frailty (loss of strength, mobility, resilience), e.g. caused by a minor stressor such as a UTI; Reduced function/deconditioning/reduced mobility; Palliative/end-of-life crisis support for symptom control and pain relief; Urgent equipment provision to make a person safe and prevent admission; Confusion/delirium (new confusion, acute worsening of dementia, excluding sepsis needing admission); Urgent catheter care (blocked catheter or catheter pain with high admission risk); Urgent support for diabetes (e.g. resolved hypoglycaemia, blood sugar concern, excluding sepsis/ketoacidosis); and unpaid carer breakdown that would otherwise cause a health crisis.

docTitle: UCR guidance
headingPath: NHS UCR guidance: Referral routes and assessment
section: NHS UCR guidance: Referral routes and assessment
text:
referral routes self-referral ambulance 999 NHS111 GP care home TEC directory of services trusted assessor prioritise schedule
Referrals come from many sources. In 2024/25 the largest routes were carer/relative (23%), self-referral (17%), community health service (16%), GP practice (15%), ambulance service (10%), care home (8%), with smaller numbers from acute hospital, A&E, hospice, local authority social services and telephone/electronic access. Referral routes include: general practice, NHS111, ambulance (999), TEC providers, community services, care homes, same day emergency care, urgent treatment centres, self/carer, local authorities, the VCFSE sector and specialist services. Providers must work with their local ambulance trust to re-direct appropriate 999 callers to UCR, and with TEC companies (pendant alarms) to provide alternatives to 999. UCR services should be profiled on the Directory of Services/NHS Service Finder. Services should have processes to prioritise and schedule people into the right care, escalate cases, and monitor call waiting and assessment times. Trusted assessor principles can reduce duplicate assessments.

docTitle: UCR guidance
headingPath: NHS UCR guidance: Assessment and intervention tools
section: NHS UCR guidance: Assessment and intervention tools
text:
assessment intervention NEWS2 observations point of care testing screening tools MUST Waterlow Braden Rockwood frailty 4AT delirium medication antibiotics catheter
Common UCR assessment activities: make the person/situation safe and consider capacity, consent and safeguarding; holistic history and review of clinical records; clinical observations including blood pressure and oxygen saturations; calculation of NEWS2 (National Early Warning Score) to identify deterioration; blood, urine and other diagnostics; equipment assessment and provision; medicines optimisation; symptom assessment; and administration of medication including nebuliser therapy. Screening tools include nutrition (MUST), pressure sore risk (Waterlow or Braden), frailty (Rockwood clinical frailty score) and delirium (4AT). Interventions in the first 48 hours may include care for nutrition, hygiene, continence, wound care, mobility and rehabilitation; pain or symptom relief; catheter care; and antibiotic prescribing including IV antibiotics as skills allow. Services should aspire to point of care testing (PoCT) at the patient's side.

docTitle: UCR guidance
headingPath: NHS UCR guidance: Workforce, skill mix and caseload
section: NHS UCR guidance: Workforce, skill mix and caseload
text:
workforce skill mix caseload registered nurses advanced clinical practitioners physiotherapists occupational therapists paramedics training frailty dementia learning disability shared records
UCR uses a flexible workforce deployed according to need, commonly including registered nurses (including district nurses), advanced clinical practitioners, physiotherapists, occupational therapists, health and care support staff, social care staff and paramedics, supported by GPs, geriatricians, mental health, learning disability and autism workers and voluntary organisations. Teams need access to advanced clinical practice, other specialties (diagnostics, phlebotomy, prescribers, 'hot clinics'), and training including comprehensive frailty training (Frailty Core Capabilities Framework), symptom assessment, dementia and mental health first aid, reasonable adjustments for learning disability and autism, and the Virtual Wards and UCR Capabilities Framework. Diagnostic tools (PoCT) and shared digital care records support care at home. Skill mix should reflect patient care needs and local requirements.

docTitle: UCR guidance
headingPath: NHS UCR guidance: Operational requirements appendix
section: NHS UCR guidance: Operational requirements appendix
text:
appendix A operational requirements ICS actions map coverage align delivery models expand hours accept referrals electronic referral clock starts stops benchmarking
Appendix A sets ICS expectations: Provide services at scale with full geographic coverage - map current coverage (including adult social care teams to avoid duplication), align delivery models across the ICS, and use demand and capacity modelling for workforce planning. Provide services 8am to 8pm seven days a week - map and expand operating hours, consult staff and unions on changes, and use modelling to prepare for expanded hours. Accept referrals from all appropriate sources - enable referrals from NHS111, 999, general practice, social care providers, ambulance clinical hubs, specialist services, care workers and local authorities; accept self-referrals; profile UCR on the local Directory of Services; and enable electronic referrals. Submit complete data to CSDS - record clinical activity per CSDS technical guidance including clock starts and stops, extract activity to CSDS, submit data for all UCR activity, and use CSDS data for benchmarking and learning.

docTitle: UCR guidance
headingPath: NHS UCR guidance: Further support and resources
section: NHS UCR guidance: Further support and resources
text:
further support resources contact email england communitycare NHS Futures collaboration platform CSDS technical guidance NICE NG74
For information and queries about UCR, email england.communitycare@nhs.net. Digital tools, resources and updates are on the Community Health Services NHS Futures collaboration platform. Key resources: CSDS technical guidance for the two-hour urgent community response standard (covering clock starts and stops); NICE guideline NG74 on referral, assessment and delivery of home-based services to reduce hospital admission; and the Urgent Community Response area on the NHS Futures platform. Publication reference PAR577; this guidance replaced the previous version published on 14 March 2022.

docTitle: Employee handbook
headingPath: Riverside Practice Employee Handbook: Joining the Practice
section: Riverside Practice Employee Handbook: Joining the Practice
text:
induction joining new starter policies patient complaints access to medical records staff as patients job description appraisal job flexibility convictions DBS disclosure
New employees complete an induction programme covering all policies and procedures. Staff must familiarise themselves with Practice policies; failure to comply could lead to disciplinary action. Patient complaints (written or verbal) must be reported to the Manager immediately and are handled per the Practice Complaints procedure. All staff can access patient medical records but access is audited and only permitted for a clinical reason or at the Doctor's discretion; records must not leave the building and you must not access family members' records without consent/permission. Staff are encouraged not to be patients of the Practice to avoid conflict of interest. A non-contractual job description is provided. Performance is monitored continuously including an appraisal scheme. Employment is conditional on a satisfactory DBS certificate; you must report any convictions or offences (including traffic offences) immediately. Disclosure information is stored securely and separately, never in the personnel file, in line with the DBS Code of Practice and Data Protection Act 2018.

docTitle: Employee handbook
headingPath: Riverside Practice Employee Handbook: Equality, inclusion and diversity
section: Riverside Practice Employee Handbook: Equality, inclusion and diversity
text:
equality inclusion diversity equal opportunity protected characteristics Equality Act 2010 discrimination cancer HIV multiple sclerosis grievance
The Practice is committed to equal opportunity. Equality means the same opportunities free from discrimination; inclusion means everyone feeling comfortable to be themselves; diversity means celebrating individual differences. The policy applies to all job applicants, employees and workers (including agency workers) across recruitment, selection, training, development and promotion. No one will receive less favourable treatment because of a protected characteristic under the Equality Act 2010: race (including colour, nationality, ethnic/national origin and caste), religion or belief, disability, sex, sexual orientation, pregnancy or maternity, gender reassignment, marriage or civil partnership, and age. Anyone diagnosed with cancer, HIV or multiple sclerosis is automatically classed as disabled. No one will be treated less favourably for trade union membership/non-membership or for being part-time or fixed-term. Discrimination can be reported to the Practice Manager or raised through the grievance procedure.

docTitle: Employee handbook
headingPath: Riverside Practice Employee Handbook: Positive work environment - harassment and bullying
section: Riverside Practice Employee Handbook: Positive work environment - harassment and bullying
text:
positive work environment harassment bullying dignity respect definition third party complaint formal informal victimisation
The Practice is committed to a harmonious, safe working environment free from harassment and bullying. Harassment is unwanted conduct related to a protected characteristic that violates dignity or creates an intimidating, hostile, degrading, humiliating or offensive environment; it can range from violence to persistently ignoring someone. Bullying is repeated inappropriate, offensive behaviour, often an abuse of power (e.g. threats, humiliation, impossible deadlines, excessive monitoring, withholding information). It is the impact, not the intent, that matters. Employees should treat others with dignity, challenge or report unacceptable behaviour and cooperate with investigations. Complaints can be informal (asking the person to stop, directly or via a manager/colleague) or formal (through the grievance procedure). Complainants are protected from victimisation; retaliation is a disciplinary offence. Harassment from third parties (clients, customers, suppliers, the public) will not be tolerated and may lead to warnings, complaints to the third party's employer, or withdrawal of services.

docTitle: Employee handbook
headingPath: Riverside Practice Employee Handbook: Positive work environment - harassment and bullying
section: Riverside Practice Employee Handbook: Positive work environment - harassment and bullying
text: Breaches are treated as misconduct, potentially gross misconduct.

docTitle: Employee handbook
headingPath: Riverside Practice Employee Handbook: Sexual harassment policy
section: Riverside Practice Employee Handbook: Sexual harassment policy
text:
sexual harassment zero tolerance victimisation confidential helper informal formal complaint third party Equality Act disciplinary Health Assured EAP
The Practice has a zero-tolerance approach to sexual harassment, which is unlawful under the Equality Act 2010. Sexual harassment is unwanted conduct of a sexual nature that violates dignity or creates an intimidating, hostile, degrading, humiliating or offensive environment; examples include sexual comments/'banter', suggestive looks, propositions, sexual gestures, intrusive questions, sexual messages or social media contact, spreading sexual rumours, and unwelcome touching. Victimisation is subjecting someone to detriment for doing a protected act. Dr R Goel has overall responsibility for the policy. Complaints can be informal (raising it with a senior colleague as a confidential helper, who cannot be the investigator) or formal (written complaint to your Manager). On a formal complaint the alleged harasser may be separated/transferred or suspended on pay; a meeting is normally held within five working days and the investigation concluded within ten working days, with a right of appeal within five working days. Third-party sexual harassment will not be tolerated.

docTitle: Employee handbook
headingPath: Riverside Practice Employee Handbook: Sexual harassment policy
section: Riverside Practice Employee Handbook: Sexual harassment policy
text: Allegations are a disciplinary matter up to summary dismissal. Support is available via the Employee Assistance Programme with Health Assured on 0800 032 7097 and the Wisdom AI wellbeing tool.

docTitle: Employee handbook
headingPath: Riverside Practice Employee Handbook: Timekeeping and time off
section: Riverside Practice Employee Handbook: Timekeeping and time off
text:
working hours timekeeping lateness appointments doctor dentist time off for dependants bereavement leave adverse weather public transport jury service
Normal hours are in your Statement; attend punctually and follow timekeeping/absence procedures. If you will be absent or late, telephone Reception and the Partner as soon as practicable. You must obtain permission before leaving early. Persistent lateness or unauthorised absence may lead to disciplinary action. Medical/dental/hospital appointments should normally be in your own time; where not practicable, time off is allowed with proof of appointment, scheduled to cause least disruption. Time off for dependants: reasonable unpaid time off for urgent/unexpected incidents involving a dependant (illness, injury, breakdown of care, death). Bereavement leave: appropriate time off and pay at the Practice's discretion on the death/funeral of a relative, civil partner or close friend. Adverse weather/transport disruption: never travel if dangerous; contact Reception and the Practice Manager; make up lost time; where attendance is impossible you normally take annual leave (or unpaid if exhausted).

docTitle: Employee handbook
headingPath: Riverside Practice Employee Handbook: Timekeeping and time off
section: Riverside Practice Employee Handbook: Timekeeping and time off
text: Jury service: notify management immediately on receiving a summons; the Practice pays the difference between court allowances and basic earnings for up to 2 weeks.

docTitle: Employee handbook
text:
pay payment itemised pay statement P60 P11D deductions overpayment income tax national insurance shortage of work short-time lay off furlough guarantee pay
Pay methods and intervals are in your Statement, with an itemised pay statement each pay period; raise queries with the Partner. The Practice makes legally required deductions such as income tax and National Insurance (with an annual P60, and P11D for non-salary benefits). Overpayments are normally recovered at the next payday, or over a longer period if it would cause hardship. The right to deduct wages is an express term of the contract. In a shortage of work the Practice may temporarily place you on short-time working (paid for hours worked), lay you off (with statutory guarantee pay), or designate you a furloughed worker under any Government scheme. These shortage-of-work provisions form part of your contractual terms.

docTitle: Employee handbook
text:
holidays annual leave entitlement holiday year 1 April 31 March booking holidays notice carry over sickness during holiday
Annual leave entitlement (including bank/public holidays) is in your Statement. The holiday year runs from 1 April to 31 March. New starters accrue 1/12th of the annual entitlement for each month of service. All holidays need prior approval; requests go to the Partner with at least 6 weeks' notice, and generally a maximum of 2 weeks may be taken at once. Where too many staff want the same period, holidays are granted first come, first served. If you fall sick before or during booked holiday you cannot retake it unless the Sickness Notification Procedure is followed with a fit note. Holidays should be taken in the current year and cannot be carried forward, with no payment in lieu except on termination. Holiday continues to accrue during Maternity, Adoption, Paternity, Shared Parental and Parental leave. During notice the Practice may direct when outstanding holiday is taken. Statutory entitlement under the Working Time Regulations 1998 is unaffected.

docTitle: Employee handbook
headingPath: Riverside Practice Employee Handbook: Sickness and statutory sick pay
section: Riverside Practice Employee Handbook: Sickness and statutory sick pay
text:
sickness absence notification procedure telephone Dr Goel infectious diseases self-certificate fit note statement of fitness for work SSP 28 weeks activity during sickness
Telephone the Practice Manager or Dr Goel on the first day of sickness, at least an hour before your start time, stating the reason and expected return, and keep in regular contact. Personal contact is required (not text/email via others). Notify the Practice of any notifiable infectious disease (e.g. mumps, measles, food poisoning) and get GP clearance before returning. Provide a Self-Certificate for absence up to 7 calendar days, and a Statement of Fitness for Work for absence over 7 days (or when requested after frequent self-certificated absence, or around holidays). Statutory Sick Pay (SSP) is not paid for the first 3 days; it usually starts on the 4th day for up to 28 weeks, paid at the current statutory rate. SSP is not payable if earnings are below the NI lower limit, for absences under 4 days, if the notification procedure is not followed, after employment ends, or while statutory maternity/adoption/paternity/shared parental pay is paid. Activity inconsistent with being unfit during sickness absence may lead to disciplinary action.

docTitle: Employee handbook
headingPath: Riverside Practice Employee Handbook: Access to medical reports
section: Riverside Practice Employee Handbook: Access to medical reports
text:
access to medical reports GP specialist occupational health consent Access to Medical Reports Act 1988 21 days amend
The Practice may need a medical report from your Doctor, Specialist or Occupational Health Provider to establish the reason for and likely duration of absence, return-to-work prospects, treatment, ability to do the job, and any reasonable adjustments. You have rights under the Access to Medical Reports Act 1988. The report cannot be released without your consent; you may withhold consent or ask to see the report first. If you wish to see it, you have 21 days to arrange access with the practitioner, can request a copy for up to 6 months, and can ask for corrections (or attach your own statement). If you withhold consent the Practice will decide on your continuing employment without medical opinion.

docTitle: Employee handbook
headingPath: Riverside Practice Employee Handbook: Benefits and Employee Assistance Programme
section: Riverside Practice Employee Handbook: Benefits and Employee Assistance Programme
text:
benefits bonus scheme employee assistance programme EAP counselling confidential wellbeing legal financial emotional
Employees are eligible for an annual bonus scheme (details issued separately). The Employee Assistance Programme (EAP) provides support on legal, financial, emotional, health and work-related issues, with details provided separately. Employee counselling is available in confidence, including for those subjected to harassment or bullying, offering guidance, informal resolution, help submitting a grievance, and counselling on future conduct, without obligation to take a complaint further.

docTitle: Employee handbook
headingPath: Riverside Practice Employee Handbook: General terms and conditions
section: Riverside Practice Employee Handbook: General terms and conditions
text:
general terms personal details other employment parking behaviour at work confidentiality copyright media inventions rights of search
Notify the Practice immediately of changes to personal details (name, address, phone, next of kin, bank details). Disclose any other or additional employment so working time implications can be discussed. Observe traffic/parking requirements; no liability for damage to private vehicles. Behave with civility; objectionable, insulting or rude behaviour or bad language may lead to disciplinary action; do not compete with the business. Do not disclose confidential or trade-secret information during or after employment; Practice documents/items must not be removed without authorisation and must be returned or destroyed on request. Written material made or acquired during employment is Practice property/copyright. Statements to the media may only be given by the Partner. Inventions made in the course of your duties may become Practice property. The Practice may search you (outer clothes only), your property, parcels, vehicles, lockers and workstations with consent and a witness, where it reasonably suspects a criminal offence or serious breach; unreasonable refusal may be treated as misconduct.

docTitle: Employee handbook
headingPath: Riverside Practice Employee Handbook: Mandatory training
section: Riverside Practice Employee Handbook: Mandatory training
text:
mandatory training statutory training updates safe working practices certificates record monitoring
You must attend statutory and mandatory training, including updates, relevant to your role to ensure safe working for you, colleagues, patients and the public. Alert the Practice Manager immediately if your training is out of date, due an update, or if a lack of training is putting anyone at risk. The Practice has a legal duty to ensure compliance; attendance is recorded for monitoring and reporting. If you cannot attend a session you are responsible for arranging the training yourself. Failure to complete mandatory training or supply certificates may lead to disciplinary action.

docTitle: Employee handbook
headingPath: Riverside Practice Employee Handbook: Confidentiality and data of the Practice
section: Riverside Practice Employee Handbook: Confidentiality and data of the Practice
text:
confidentiality sensitive information staffing patients database policies technical data unauthorised media contact gross misconduct
You must not, during or after employment, use or disclose confidential or sensitive information about the Practice's business or trade secrets, or about individuals connected with the Practice (employees, contractors, patients), unless for legitimate business interests or with written authorisation. Confidential/sensitive information includes details about staffing; patients and employees (actual, potential and past); the Practice's databases; planning; policies; rules and manuals; technical data; research; and testing. Unless authorised, you must not contact the media or discuss your employment or Practice business with non-employees. Disclosing or misusing this information is treated as gross misconduct and may lead to termination without notice or pay in lieu.

docTitle: Employee handbook
headingPath: Riverside Practice Employee Handbook: Safeguarding policy
section: Riverside Practice Employee Handbook: Safeguarding policy
text:
safeguarding children vulnerable adults abuse neglect physical sexual emotional financial designated safeguarding officer report disclosure code of conduct
The Practice is committed to safeguarding children and vulnerable adults. Abuse and neglect can be by omission or commission; main categories are neglect, physical, sexual, emotional and financial abuse. There is a designated Safeguarding Officer responsible for implementing the policy. Every employee must report suspected abuse to the Safeguarding Officer as soon as possible - report only, never investigate (that is the role of the Police and Children's Services). Provide an oral then written report, recording concerns/observations/persons present, signed, timed and dated; if abuse is disclosed, record the facts in the person's own words without judgement and make clear early that you must report it. Only those who need to know should be told. The code of conduct includes: do not spend excessive time alone with children/vulnerable adults, do not take them alone in a car or to your home, no inappropriate touching, no over-familiar or sexually suggestive comments, no inappropriate computer/phone activity, treat everyone with respect, and challenge and report all concerns to the safeguarding officer.

docTitle: Employee handbook
headingPath: Riverside Practice Employee Handbook: Administering drugs
section: Riverside Practice Employee Handbook: Administering drugs
text:
administering drugs medication record dosage security Medicines Act 1968 Misuse of Drugs Act 1974 unqualified report missing drugs
If you handle or administer drugs you must comply with the procedures and the Medicines Act 1968 and Misuse of Drugs Act 1974. You must: accurately record medicines and update records at the time of administration (type of drug, dosage, when issued and by whom); never leave medicines or drugs unattended and be responsible for their security; not administer misleading or unclear prescriptions without clarification; never administer drugs if unqualified unless supervised by a senior qualified member of staff; report any missing drugs immediately; and never exceed the stated dosage without a Doctor's written consent. Failure to comply may result in a disciplinary warning or dismissal.

docTitle: Employee handbook
headingPath: Riverside Practice Employee Handbook: Maternity rights
section: Riverside Practice Employee Handbook: Maternity rights
text:
pregnancy maternity antenatal care risk assessment maternity leave 52 weeks OML AML MAT B1 KIT days holidays statutory maternity pay SMP
Pregnant employees have statutory rights. Antenatal care: reasonable paid time off for appointments (with a certificate of pregnancy and appointment card if requested). A maternity risk assessment is carried out once the employee notifies in writing that she is pregnant, has given birth in the last six months, or is breastfeeding. Maternity leave: up to 52 weeks if you notify in writing by the 15th week before the Expected Week of Childbirth (EWC), submitting a MAT B1 form and the EWC and intended start date. Two weeks' compulsory leave applies immediately after birth. All contractual benefits except pay continue. Give at least 8 weeks' notice to return early; returning at the end of Ordinary Maternity Leave (first 26 weeks) gives a right to the same job, while returning during/after Additional Maternity Leave (second 26 weeks) may be the same or a suitable alternative job. Up to 10 Keeping in Touch (KIT) days are allowed without ending leave. Holiday accrues throughout.

docTitle: Employee handbook
headingPath: Riverside Practice Employee Handbook: Maternity rights
section: Riverside Practice Employee Handbook: Maternity rights
text: Statutory Maternity Pay (SMP) is payable for up to 39 weeks if qualifying: 90% of earnings for the first 6 weeks, then the statutory rate (or 90% if lower) for 33 weeks; the final 13 weeks are unpaid.

docTitle: Employee handbook
headingPath: Riverside Practice Employee Handbook: Paternity leave and pay
section: Riverside Practice Employee Handbook: Paternity leave and pay
text:
paternity leave eligibility 26 weeks ante-natal appointments two weeks notice notification statutory paternity pay SPP shared parental leave
Paternity leave supports fathers/partners of a pregnant person or joint adopters (secondary adopter). You can take unpaid time off to attend up to 2 ante-natal/adoption appointments (max 6.5 hours each). Eligibility: continuously employed at least 26 weeks by the end of the 15th week before the EWC (or by the week of being matched for adoption); be the father or the mother's/adopter's spouse, civil partner or partner; and have responsibility for the child's upbringing. Leave is one or two weeks (consecutive or two non-consecutive single weeks), taken within 52 weeks of birth/placement. Notice of entitlement is required by the 15th week before the EWC (or within 7 days of being matched for adoption), with at least 28 days' notice of leave dates. Statutory Paternity Pay (SPP) is payable for up to 2 weeks at the standard rate if you meet the earnings and relationship criteria. On return from an isolated paternity leave you are entitled to the same job. Shared parental leave may also be available.

docTitle: Employee handbook
headingPath: Riverside Practice Employee Handbook: Adoption leave and pay
section: Riverside Practice Employee Handbook: Adoption leave and pay
text:
adoption leave appointments matched placement 52 weeks ordinary additional KIT days statutory adoption pay SAP notification
If matched with a child for adoption you may take paid time off for up to 5 pre-placement appointments (primary adopter); secondary adopters get unpaid time off for up to 2 appointments (max 6.5 hours each). Adoption leave is up to 52 weeks if qualifying. Notify the Practice within 7 days of being matched, giving the expected placement date, intended start and length of leave, with documentary evidence if asked. Leave can start from placement or up to 14 days before. All contractual benefits except pay continue. Give at least 8 weeks' notice to return early; returning after Ordinary Adoption Leave (first 26 weeks) gives a right to the same job, while after Additional Adoption Leave (second 26 weeks) it may be the same or a suitable alternative. Up to 10 KIT days are allowed. Statutory Adoption Pay (SAP) is payable for up to 39 weeks if qualifying: 90% of earnings for 6 weeks then the statutory rate (or 90% if lower) for 33 weeks; the final 13 weeks are unpaid.

docTitle: Employee handbook
headingPath: Riverside Practice Employee Handbook: Shared parental and parental leave
section: Riverside Practice Employee Handbook: Shared parental and parental leave
text:
shared parental leave SPL pay 50 weeks SPLIT days parental leave 18 weeks unpaid eligibility one year service
Shared Parental Leave (SPL): eligible parents can share up to 50 weeks' leave and up to 37 weeks' pay after the mother/primary adopter ends their maternity/adoption leave, taken in a continuous block or discontinuous periods, at the same or different times. Up to 20 Shared Parental Leave in Touch (SPLIT) days are allowed without ending leave. The rules are complex - discuss with your Manager. Parental Leave: with 1 year's continuous service, parents/those with parental responsibility can take up to 18 weeks' unpaid Ordinary Parental Leave per child up to the child's 18th birthday, in minimum blocks of 1 week (single days if the child is disabled), limited to 4 weeks per child per year, with at least 21 days' notice; leave may be postponed depending on the needs of the Practice.

docTitle: Employee handbook
headingPath: Riverside Practice Employee Handbook: Neonatal care leave
section: Riverside Practice Employee Handbook: Neonatal care leave
text:
neonatal care leave entitlement tier 1 tier 2 12 weeks 7 consecutive days 28 days statutory neonatal care pay notice 68 weeks
Employees have a day-one statutory right to neonatal care leave when responsible for a child receiving neonatal care. It applies where the child receives neonatal care lasting at least 7 consecutive days starting within 28 days of birth. You are entitled to one week of leave per consecutive 7-day period of care, up to a maximum of 12 weeks. Leave is taken in two periods: tier 1 (from when care starts until 7 days after it ends - can be non-consecutive, notified verbally before your first day of leave) and tier 2 (outside tier 1 - must be consecutive, with 15 days' notice for a single week or 28 days for two or more weeks). Leave must be taken in minimum blocks of one week, before 68 weeks from the child's birth/placement. Statutory neonatal care pay (up to 12 weeks) is payable if you have 26 weeks' service and meet the earnings criteria, at the lower of the statutory rate or 90% of weekly earnings. You return to the same job (or a similar job on no less favourable terms after a certain point).

docTitle: Employee handbook
headingPath: Riverside Practice Employee Handbook: Parental bereavement leave
section: Riverside Practice Employee Handbook: Parental bereavement leave
text:
parental bereavement leave death of a child under 18 stillbirth 24 weeks two weeks 56 weeks statutory parental bereavement pay notification
Parental Bereavement Leave is available from day one on the death of a child under 18, or a stillbirth after 24 weeks of pregnancy. Eligible people include natural, adoptive and 'foster to adopt' parents, certain natural parents with a contact order, intended parents under surrogacy, a 'parent in fact' (the child lived with them for at least 4 weeks with day-to-day responsibility, including guardians and foster parents but not paid carers), and the partner of any of these. You may take two weeks total, as a single one- or two-week block or two separate weeks, taken any time within 56 weeks of the death. For leave within the first 56 days no advance notice is needed (telephone your manager); after 56 days give one week's notice. Statutory Parental Bereavement Pay is payable if you have 26 weeks' service and meet the earnings criteria, at the statutory rate or 90% of weekly earnings (whichever is lower). Contractual terms (except pay) continue and you have a right to return to the same job. A 24-hour telephone counselling service is available.

docTitle: Employee handbook
headingPath: Riverside Practice Employee Handbook: Flexible working policy
section: Riverside Practice Employee Handbook: Flexible working policy
text:
flexible working request job share part-time annualised compressed hours flexitime term-time home working business need eligibility appeal two months
The Practice supports flexible working to improve work-life balance while maintaining staffing in line with business demands. Options include job-sharing, part-time, annualised hours, compressed hours, flexitime, term-time working, swapping hours, working from home, career breaks and flexible shift working. Not all patterns suit all roles; requests are assessed on cost, effect on service delivery, supervision needs, department structure and workload. You can make a statutory request from day one of employment, up to two requests in any 12-month period. Applications must be in writing, dated, specify the change and proposed start date, state any previous application, and say whether it is requested as a reasonable adjustment under the Equality Act 2010. The Practice consults on the application, notifies the decision, and offers a right of appeal. Accepted requests are a permanent contract variation. Applications are concluded within two months unless an extension is agreed; you may be accompanied at meetings.

docTitle: Employee handbook
headingPath: Riverside Practice Employee Handbook: Carer's leave policy
section: Riverside Practice Employee Handbook: Carer's leave policy
text:
carers leave unpaid one week dependant long-term care need day one statutory notice postpone
Employees have a day-one statutory right to unpaid carer's leave: one working week per rolling 12-month period to provide or arrange care for a dependant with a long-term care need. It can be taken in a continuous block or separate occurrences of at least half a working day. A dependant is a spouse/civil partner, child, parent, someone living in the same household (not a tenant/lodger/boarder/employee) or someone who reasonably relies on you for care (e.g. an elderly neighbour). A long-term care need means an illness/injury likely to require care for more than three months, a disability under the Equality Act 2010, or care connected with old age. No evidence is required. Requests must be in writing specifying entitlement and the days/part-days; notice is double the time requested or three days, whichever is longer. The Practice may postpone leave by up to one month if the business would be unduly disrupted, confirming the reason and new date in writing. Carer's leave is unpaid.

docTitle: Employee handbook
headingPath: Riverside Practice Employee Handbook: Standards - dress code and wastage
section: Riverside Practice Employee Handbook: Standards - dress code and wastage
text:
dress code professional appearance uniform faith clothing wastage minimum waste damage negligence deduction
Dress code: present a professional, neat and clean appearance with clothing and footwear appropriate to your work. Casual t-shirts, vests and shorts are not acceptable. Requirements of particular faiths to wear specific clothing or dress modestly are respected provided they pose no health and safety hazard. Any issued uniform must be worn. Wastage: the Practice maintains a policy of minimum waste - handle equipment and stock with care, turn off unnecessary lighting/heating, keep doors closed, and minimise delay. Any damage to vehicles, stock or property from your carelessness, negligence or vandalism, or any loss from failing to observe rules or from negligent/unsatisfactory work, may make you liable to pay the cost; the Practice may deduct such costs from your pay.

docTitle: Employee handbook
headingPath: Riverside Practice Employee Handbook: Anti-bribery policy
section: Riverside Practice Employee Handbook: Anti-bribery policy
text:
anti-bribery zero tolerance Bribery Act 2010 hospitality business gifts facilitation payments political donations penalties anti-bribery officer
The Practice has zero tolerance towards bribery and carries out business fairly, honestly and openly. All employees must prevent, detect and report bribery. The Practice carries out risk assessments, maintains internal controls and record-keeping, trains staff to recognise bribery, and has clear reporting procedures. Concerns should be raised with your Manager or the Partner and are treated in confidence (anonymously if appropriate). It is prohibited to offer, give, request or receive any advantage to gain an improper advantage, or to bribe a UK or foreign public official. Hospitality and business gifts: must be reasonable and proportionate and referred to/agreed by the anti-bribery officer; records must be kept; cash gifts, quid pro quo arrangements, sexual entertainment, facilitation payments and political donations are expressly prohibited; small promotional items (diaries, pens) and modest meals to build business relationships are generally acceptable. Penalties under the Bribery Act 2010 include unlimited fines and imprisonment; breaches may lead to disciplinary action.

docTitle: Employee handbook
headingPath: Riverside Practice Employee Handbook: Data protection policy
section: Riverside Practice Employee Handbook: Data protection policy
text:
data protection GDPR personal data special categories lawful basis subject access request data security breach 72 hours retention compliance officer Rajiv Goel
The Practice handles personal information lawfully and correctly, adhering to the GDPR. The policy covers personal data of job applicants, employees, former employees, apprentices, volunteers, placement students, workers and contractors. Data protection principles require processing to be fair, lawful and transparent; collected for specific, legitimate purposes; adequate, relevant and limited to what is necessary; accurate and up to date; not kept longer than necessary; and kept secure. Special categories include health, sex life, sexual orientation, race, ethnic origin, political opinion, religion, trade union membership, and genetic/biometric data. Individuals have rights including to be informed, of access, rectification, erasure, restriction, portability, objection, and regarding automated decision-making. Processing needs a lawful basis. Subject Access Requests are answered without delay and within one month (extendable), normally free of charge.

docTitle: Employee handbook
headingPath: Riverside Practice Employee Handbook: Data protection policy
section: Riverside Practice Employee Handbook: Data protection policy
text: Data must be kept securely (locked storage, encryption, passwords, screen locks); personal data should not be kept on laptops/USB sticks without authorisation. Data breaches are recorded on the Data Breach Register and, where required, reported to the Information Commissioner within 72 hours. The data protection compliance officer is Rajiv Goel (rajiv.goel@nhs.net).

docTitle: Employee handbook
headingPath: Riverside Practice Employee Handbook: IT and communications
section: Riverside Practice Employee Handbook: IT and communications
text:
IT communications monitoring email internet passwords personal use software download virus tracking personal mobile phones
The Practice may access and monitor the use of all Practice-owned digital devices, including internet, telephone and email, and monitors access to its networks via private devices. Guard against unauthorised access, alteration, loss, disclosure or destruction of data; never divulge your password or exceed your authorised access; you are responsible for activity in your accounts. Personal use of computer and telephone systems is not permitted (personal calls only in exceptional circumstances with your Manager's agreement), and personal files/software/apps must not be stored on Practice systems. Do not access illegal, pornographic or hateful/discriminatory content or send offensive communications. Do not photograph or film others without consent. Email is legally binding. Do not download or load software from any source without management approval and virus checking; downloading reputable free software is permitted for a valid business reason. Do not make pirate copies of Practice software. Devices may contain tracking facilities (for theft prevention, health and safety, and checking records); tampering may lead to summary dismissal.

docTitle: Employee handbook
headingPath: Riverside Practice Employee Handbook: IT and communications
section: Riverside Practice Employee Handbook: IT and communications
text: Personal mobiles should normally be turned off during working hours unless approved; unauthorised use may lead to disciplinary action. Monitoring of personal communications is for work-related purposes only and forms part of contractual terms.

docTitle: Employee handbook
headingPath: Riverside Practice Employee Handbook: Social media policy
section: Riverside Practice Employee Handbook: Social media policy
text:
social media personal accounts reputation confidential information disciplinary dismissal privacy settings
Personal social media accounts must only be used to express personal views, with care taken where you are identifiable as a Practice employee; you must identify yourself as an employee when referencing Practice products or services. Refrain from comments or discussions that could adversely affect the Practice or its reputation, or that of customers/suppliers; do not breach discrimination law, harass or bully an employee, or damage working relationships. Do not share confidential or sensitive Practice information. You are personally responsible for all content posted; keep passwords secure. Everything posted online can become public regardless of privacy settings. Breaching this policy may lead to disciplinary action up to and including dismissal - including posts made outside working hours or not on Practice equipment - and you may be required to remove content. Read alongside the equality and positive work environment policies.

docTitle: Employee handbook
headingPath: Riverside Practice Employee Handbook: Grievance procedure
section: Riverside Practice Employee Handbook: Grievance procedure
text:
grievance procedure informal formal writing meeting 5 days accompanied appeal recording
Raise a grievance about any aspect of your employment informally first (your Statement says with whom). A formal grievance must be in writing. Grievances are considered as soon as possible, with a meeting usually within 5 days where you give full details. You may be accompanied by a fellow employee or accredited trade union official. If the grievance is about the person you would normally raise it with, raise it with a more senior manager (or one at the same level). The Manager informs you of the decision in writing, and you may appeal in writing within 5 working days, after which you attend another meeting and are told the final decision in writing. Recording meetings (audio or visual) without express written authorisation is not permitted and may lead to disciplinary action including dismissal for gross misconduct.

docTitle: Employee handbook
headingPath: Riverside Practice Employee Handbook: Whistleblowing - public interest disclosure
section: Riverside Practice Employee Handbook: Whistleblowing - public interest disclosure
text:
whistleblowing public interest disclosure protected disclosure criminal offence legal obligation health and safety detriment Protect prescribed person
The Practice encourages honest communication so concerns can be addressed. Anyone raising genuine concerns in the public interest will not be subjected to detriment; victimisation of someone making a protected disclosure is a disciplinary offence. The policy applies where you reasonably believe (and disclosure is in the public interest) that a criminal offence, failure to comply with a legal obligation, miscarriage of justice, danger to health and safety, environmental damage, or concealment of these has occurred, is occurring or is likely. You need only a reasonable suspicion, not proof. Procedure: raise the issue with the Practice Manager (or a more senior/equal-level manager if not appropriate); an investigation may follow and you may attend an investigatory meeting (you can bring a colleague); you will be advised of the outcome where possible. If dissatisfied, raise it with the Partner, then a relevant Prescribed Person under the Public Interest Disclosure (Prescribed Persons) Order 2014, or Protect.

docTitle: Employee handbook
headingPath: Riverside Practice Employee Handbook: Whistleblowing - public interest disclosure
section: Riverside Practice Employee Handbook: Whistleblowing - public interest disclosure
text: Disclosures not substantially believed to be true, made in bad faith or for personal gain may lead to disciplinary action including dismissal.

docTitle: Employee handbook
headingPath: Riverside Practice Employee Handbook: Health, safety and hygiene
section: Riverside Practice Employee Handbook: Health, safety and hygiene
text:
health safety hygiene accident book smoke free workplace e-cigarettes cuts dressing infectious contagious disease
The Practice is committed to your health, safety and welfare. Report any potential hazard or unsafe condition; take reasonable steps to safeguard yourself and others; observe health, safety and fire rules; and report all accidents to management and the Accident Book. All workplaces are smoke-free (including e-cigarettes/ENDS) with no exceptions; you are personally liable for any fine for smoking in a smoke-free place, and breaches may lead to disciplinary action. Hygiene: cover any exposed cut or burn with a first-aid dressing; if suffering from an infectious or contagious disease (e.g. rubella, hepatitis) do not report for work without GP clearance and report any contact with such illness before commencing work.

docTitle: Employee handbook
headingPath: Riverside Practice Employee Handbook: Alcohol, substance abuse and mental health
section: Riverside Practice Employee Handbook: Alcohol, substance abuse and mental health
text:
alcohol substance abuse misuse health problem occupational health confidential supporting positive mental health reasonable adjustments EAP counselling
Alcohol and Substance Abuse: the Practice treats alcohol/substance misuse as a health problem, promotes responsible attitudes, offers assistance and arranges professional help. Absence due to alcohol/substance abuse is treated like sickness absence provided you obtain professional treatment and keep in contact with Occupational Health; discussions are confidential. Persistent inadequate performance or unacceptable behaviour may be dealt with under the Disciplinary Procedure, and gross misconduct remains a dismissible offence; failing to complete treatment or relapsing may also be addressed under that procedure. Supporting Positive Mental Health: the Practice provides support for employees with mental health problems, creates a safe workplace, and makes reasonable adjustments for disabled employees under the Equality Act 2010. Managers will intervene early, hold confidential meetings to identify support and adjustments, and encourage use of the confidential telephone counselling service via the Employee Assistance Programme.

docTitle: Employee handbook
headingPath: Riverside Practice Employee Handbook: Disciplinary rules and gross misconduct
section: Riverside Practice Employee Handbook: Disciplinary rules and gross misconduct
text:
disciplinary rules general rules conduct standards gross misconduct summary dismissal fighting theft drugs alcohol discrimination harassment recording
Disciplinary rules set standards of performance and behaviour and aim to encourage improvement, not just punishment. General rules (non-exhaustive) require you to: conduct yourself in the Practice's interests and avoid damaging its image; maintain a good standard of work, attendance and timekeeping; observe authorised notices; undertake reasonable duties; not use communications for personal purposes without permission; not remove material/equipment without permission; report damage; not do unauthorised work using Practice time/equipment; maintain high personal hygiene; not allow unauthorised visitors; behave courteously; submit to searches; comply with no-smoking rules; and not display offensive material or give unofficial references.

docTitle: Employee handbook
headingPath: Riverside Practice Employee Handbook: Disciplinary rules and gross misconduct
section: Riverside Practice Employee Handbook: Disciplinary rules and gross misconduct
text: Gross misconduct (examples, non-exhaustive) may lead to summary dismissal without notice or prior warnings, including serious instances of: fighting, physical assault or dangerous horseplay; serious bullying, threatening or intimidating behaviour or excessive bad language; theft, misappropriation or fraud; being under the influence of alcohol/drugs or drug abuse at work; possessing or dealing illegal drugs at work; breach of safety rules endangering others; unlawful discrimination, harassment or bullying; breach of any Practice policy; deliberate damage to property; and unauthorised recording of any workplace meeting.

docTitle: Employee handbook
headingPath: Riverside Practice Employee Handbook: Disciplinary, capability and appeal procedures
section: Riverside Practice Employee Handbook: Disciplinary, capability and appeal procedures
text:
disciplinary procedure capability procedure suspension warnings verbal written final dismissal appeal 5 working days accompanied
The Disciplinary and Capability procedures do not form part of the contract; the Practice has discretion to vary them for length of service (short-service employees may not receive warnings before dismissal). Disciplinary Procedure: facts are established first; you may be suspended on full pay during investigation; you receive a written statement of the concern and a formal meeting (with the right to be accompanied by a fellow employee or accredited trade union official) before any warning or dismissal. Levels are verbal warning (held 6 months), written warning (12 months), final written warning (12 months), and dismissal (with or without notice, whether or not warnings were issued). Capability Procedure handles deterioration in ability to do the job (job changes, or health/personal circumstances), normally with informal discussion and time to improve, medical reports where relevant, and the same warning levels. Recording meetings without written authorisation is not permitted and may be gross misconduct.

docTitle: Employee handbook
headingPath: Riverside Practice Employee Handbook: Disciplinary, capability and appeal procedures
section: Riverside Practice Employee Handbook: Disciplinary, capability and appeal procedures
text: Appeals against any disciplinary or capability decision must be made in writing within 5 working days; you may be accompanied, and you are told the final decision after the appeal meeting.

docTitle: Employee handbook
headingPath: Riverside Practice Employee Handbook: Termination of employment
section: Riverside Practice Employee Handbook: Termination of employment
text:
termination notice resignation gross misconduct probationary period dismissal notice effect retirement return of property pay in lieu garden leave
Resignations must be in writing giving the notice in your Statement; the Practice gives the notice in your Statement if it terminates your employment. You may be summarily dismissed without notice for gross misconduct. Notice during a probationary period may differ (see your Statement). Verbal dismissal notice takes effect immediately; posted notice takes effect the day after an email, two days after recorded/special delivery, or three days after first class. The Practice does not operate a formal retirement policy. If you leave without giving/working the required notice, the additional cost of covering your duties may be deducted from termination pay and you forfeit contractual (above statutory) accrued holiday pay. You must return all Practice property on termination or have its cost deducted. The Practice may, at its discretion, pay in lieu of notice, and may place you on garden leave (on full salary and benefits) during the notice period.
